title: Implement centralized authentication and authorization (OAuth2/OIDC, JWT validation, RBAC) across all services
description: Deploy a centralized authentication and authorization system for all API endpoints and services. Integrate an OAuth2/OIDC provider (e.g., Keycloak, Auth0, or Zitadel) for user and service account management. Implement JWT validation middleware in each backend service (Rust, Go, Node.js) and enforce application-level RBAC based on roles and permissions in JWT claims. Harden the Morgan agent web chat by adding tool-call authorization and abuse prevention. Ensure all endpoints are protected by authentication and authorization gates, and anonymous access is only allowed where explicitly required.
subtasks: Deploy and configure an OAuth2/OIDC provider (Keycloak/Auth0/Zitadel) for user and service account management
Define application roles (admin, sales, operations, finance, viewer) and permissions model
Implement JWT validation middleware in all backend services (Rust, Go, Node.js)
Integrate user authentication in the Next.js frontend and Morgan web chat
Implement RBAC middleware in each service to enforce endpoint-level permissions based on JWT claims
Issue service account tokens for inter-service calls and configure trust relationships
Harden Morgan agent web chat: add prompt injection defenses, per-session/IP conversation limits, and tool-call authorization
Update documentation to reflect authentication and authorization requirements
Run end-to-end authentication/authorization tests across all endpoints
Run OWASP ZAP scan against all API endpoints to verify authentication enforcement
acceptance_criteria: All API endpoints (including Equipment Catalog, RMS, Finance, Vetting, Social Media Engine, Morgan web chat) require valid JWTs for access unless explicitly public
RBAC model is enforced at the application level, restricting access to sensitive endpoints based on user roles
Morgan agent web chat cannot trigger sensitive tool calls (e.g., credit checks, invoice creation) without authentication
Prompt injection and abuse prevention controls are in place for the Morgan agent
End-to-end tests confirm unauthorized requests are rejected with 401/403
OWASP ZAP scan shows no unauthenticated access to protected endpoints

title: Implement encryption at rest for sensitive data and secure Signal integration
description: Enable encryption at rest for all sensitive data in PostgreSQL and for the Signal-CLI persistent volume. Use PostgreSQL TDE via the CloudNative-PG operator or implement application-level encryption for PII columns. Store encryption keys in a secure KMS or Vault, separate from the data. For Signal integration, minimize sensitive data sent via Signal, ensure the Signal-CLI PVC is encrypted, and restrict access to the Signal-CLI REST API.
subtasks: Enable PostgreSQL Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) via CloudNative-PG operator settings
Identify and implement application-level encryption for PII columns (names, emails, phones, credit data) if TDE is not available
Integrate a key management solution (KMS or Vault) for encryption key storage and rotation
Encrypt the Signal-CLI persistent volume (PVC) at rest
Update Signal integration to send only notification links, not full sensitive content
Restrict network access to the Signal-CLI REST API to only authorized services
Implement message retention policies for Signal-CLI
Audit and document all data flows involving PII and sensitive business data
Test encryption at rest by verifying data is unreadable without keys
Run a data exfiltration simulation to confirm encryption effectiveness
acceptance_criteria: All sensitive fields in PostgreSQL (PII, financial data, credit data) are encrypted at rest
Encryption keys are stored and rotated securely, separate from the data
Signal-CLI PVC is encrypted and access is restricted
No sensitive business data is sent via Signal except for notification links
Data export from storage is unreadable without decryption keys
Penetration test confirms data at rest is protected

title: Implement GDPR compliance: data export, consent management, and scheduled hard deletion
description: Implement GDPR compliance features across all services handling PII. Add data export API endpoints, consent tracking, and a scheduled job for hard-deleting records past retention. Create a data classification document mapping all PII fields and ensure audit logging covers all PII access.
subtasks: Implement data export API endpoints in each service for right of access and data portability
Implement consent tracking and management for all user/customer data
Create a scheduled job/process for hard-deleting records past retention period
Develop a data classification and flow mapping document for all PII fields and data flows
Ensure audit logging is enabled for all PII access and modification events
Test data export and deletion flows for completeness and correctness
Review and update privacy policy documentation to reflect implemented controls
acceptance_criteria: Users can request and receive a complete export of their data
Consent is tracked and can be revoked for all PII processing
Records are hard-deleted after retention period, with audit logs of deletion
Data classification document is complete and up to date
Audit logs show all PII access and modification events
GDPR compliance is verified by a simulated data subject request

title: Enforce secure secret management and eliminate plaintext secrets in source control
description: Replace all plaintext Kubernetes Secrets and placeholder secret manifests in source control with SealedSecrets or External Secrets Operator. Implement a GitOps-compatible secret management workflow and ensure no secret manifests with placeholder values are ever committed. Move secret management decision (dp-15) earlier in the deployment timeline.
subtasks: Remove all plaintext and placeholder secret manifests from source control
Deploy SealedSecrets or External Secrets Operator cluster-wide
Migrate all existing secrets to the new encrypted or external secret management system
Implement a secret provisioning script or workflow for operators
Update documentation and onboarding guides to reflect new secret management process
Audit the repository to ensure no secrets or placeholders remain
Test secret rotation and recovery using the new system
acceptance_criteria: No plaintext or placeholder secrets are present in any repository
All secrets are managed via SealedSecrets or External Secrets Operator
Secret rotation and recovery processes are documented and tested
Repository audit confirms no accidental secret exposure
CI/CD pipeline enforces secret management policy

title: Harden input validation and file upload security across all services
description: Implement comprehensive input validation for all API endpoints and file upload handlers. Define and enforce max field lengths, regex patterns, barcode format validation, and file upload restrictions (max size, allowed MIME types, magic byte verification). Add malware scanning, image bomb protection, and EXIF metadata stripping for file uploads. Require parameterized queries in all database access.
subtasks: Define and enforce input validation schemas for all endpoints (max lengths, regex, required fields)
Implement barcode format validation and file upload restrictions (max size, allowed MIME types, magic byte verification)
Integrate malware scanning (e.g., ClamAV) for uploaded files
Implement image dimension limits and EXIF metadata stripping for image uploads
Require parameterized queries in all database access and enforce via code review
Add fuzzing/property-based tests for critical input paths
Run security testing (e.g., OWASP ZAP, fuzzing) against all endpoints
acceptance_criteria: All endpoints reject invalid or malicious input with clear error messages
File uploads are restricted by size, type, and content, and are scanned for malware
No EXIF metadata is present in stored images
All database queries use parameterized statements
Fuzzing and security scans show no input validation or injection vulnerabilities

title: Implement secure infrastructure configuration: ConfigMap scoping, database TLS, and S3/R2 bucket hardening
description: Refactor ConfigMap usage to provide per-service endpoint scoping and restrict access via Kubernetes RBAC. Enable TLS for all database (PostgreSQL, Redis) connections and enforce client-side verification. Harden S3/R2 bucket policies to restrict public access, enforce CORS for specific domains, and use pre-signed URLs for uploads.
subtasks: Refactor ConfigMaps to provide per-service endpoint scoping; remove monolithic ConfigMap replication
Restrict ConfigMap access to only intended service accounts using Kubernetes RBAC
Enable and enforce TLS/SSL for PostgreSQL and Redis at both server and client sides
Update all service connection strings to require TLS (sslmode=verify-full for PostgreSQL)
Configure S3/R2 bucket policies to deny public listing and restrict uploads to pre-signed URLs
Restrict CORS origins to specific domains and audit bucket ACLs
Test all service connections for TLS enforcement and bucket access for correct policy
acceptance_criteria: Each service can only access its own ConfigMap endpoints
All database and cache connections use TLS with certificate verification
S3/R2 buckets do not allow public listing or unauthorized uploads
CORS is restricted to approved domains
Security tests confirm no unauthorized access to infrastructure endpoints or storage

title: Establish application-level security event logging and supply chain security controls
description: Define a security event taxonomy and implement structured security logging for authentication failures, authorization denials, rate limit violations, input validation failures, and suspicious activity. Integrate dependency and container image scanning into CI/CD pipelines, and generate SBOMs for all services.
subtasks: Define a security event taxonomy for all services
Implement structured security logging for all relevant security events
Integrate security logs with the alerting system for real-time monitoring
Add dependency vulnerability scanning (cargo audit, npm audit/snyk, govulncheck) to CI/CD pipelines
Integrate container image scanning (Trivy or Grype) into build pipelines
Generate and publish SBOMs for all service builds
Pin all dependency versions in lock files and use minimal base images
Test logging and scanning by introducing known vulnerabilities and verifying detection
acceptance_criteria: All security-relevant events are logged in a structured, queryable format
Security logs are integrated with alerting and monitoring systems
CI/CD pipelines fail on critical dependency or image vulnerabilities
SBOMs are available for all deployed services
Penetration test confirms security event logging and supply chain controls are effective

title: Implement CSRF protection and document anti-CSRF strategy for all state-changing endpoints
description: Implement CSRF protection for all state-changing endpoints (POST, PUT, DELETE) if cookie-based sessions are used. If using Bearer tokens, ensure tokens are never stored in cookies. For approval links (e.g., Signal), use single-use tokens with short TTLs. Document the anti-CSRF strategy for all services.
subtasks: Implement CSRF token validation middleware for all state-changing endpoints using cookies
Ensure Bearer tokens are not stored in cookies if Bearer-only auth is used
Implement single-use, short-lived tokens for approval links (e.g., Signal approval/rejection)
Document the anti-CSRF strategy for all services and endpoints
Test CSRF protection by attempting cross-site requests and verifying rejection
acceptance_criteria: All state-changing endpoints are protected against CSRF attacks
Approval links are single-use and expire after a short TTL
Documentation clearly describes the anti-CSRF approach
Security testing confirms CSRF protection is effective
